macro_segments.title: The Duration of Childbirth Pains (Kishuy) and Its Effect on Blood Status

micro_segments.title: Introducing the Question: How Many Days Back Can Childbirth Pains Affect Blood Status?
The Debate Between Rabbi Meir (40-50 Days), Rabbi Yehuda (One Month), and Others (Two Weeks)
Resolving the Forty vs. Fifty Days Question: Sick vs. Healthy Women
Rabbi Levi's Position: Childbirth Pains Work Only During Zavah (Not Niddah) Days
Rava's Expansion: Including Days Relevant to Zavah Counting (Days 8-25)
Rav Adda bar Ahava's Radical Extension: Zavah Status Continues Indefinitely After Initial Bleeding
Reconciling Forty-Fifty Days with the Eleven-Day Zavah Period

macro_segments.title: Mathematical Calculations: How Long Can a Woman Bleed Without Becoming Zavah?

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Meir's Scenario: 150 Days of Bleeding Without Becoming Zavah
The Precise Count: 2 + 7 + 2 + 50 Days Before Birth, Plus 80 Days After Birth
Adding Post-Birth Days: The Complete 150-Day Calculation
The Challenge: Could a Woman Bleed Every Day of Her Life Without Becoming Zavah?
The Response: Childbirth Pains (Kishuy) Do Not Apply to Miscarriages

macro_segments.title: The Baraita's More Modest Count: 100 Days of Bleeding Without Becoming Zavah

micro_segments.title: The Calculation: 2 + 7 + 2 + 80 + 7 + 2 Days Without Childbirth Pains
The Point: Proving That Opening of the Womb Does Not Necessarily Cause Bleeding

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yehuda's Position: The Ninth Month (Calendar Month) Is Determinative

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yehuda's Lenient Standard: Any Bleeding with Pain in the Ninth Month Is Permitted
The Strict Application: Most Bleeding in the Eighth Month Makes Her Zavah
The Leniency Application: Most Bleeding in the Ninth Month Is Always Permitted
Rav Adda bar Ahava's Inference: The Calendar Month (Shofar) Determines the Halacha

macro_segments.title: The Biology of Pregnancy: Shmuel's Precise Calculation and the Chasidim's Timing

micro_segments.title: Shmuel's Calculation: Exactly 271 Days of Pregnancy (Or 272-273 from Intercourse)
The Chasidim's Strategy: Only Having Intercourse Wednesday to Avoid Shabbat Birth
The Mathematical Proof: Why Wednesday-Friday Were Safe Days
The Gematria Source: The Word Herayon (Pregnancy) Equals 271
Seventh-Month Birth: Even Viable Births Can Occur at the Beginning of the Seventh Month

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yosi and Rabbi Shimon's Restrictive Position: Only Two Weeks of Kishuy

micro_segments.title: The Limitation: Childbirth Pains Work Only for Two Weeks Before Birth
The Source: The Verse on the Yoledet States Two Weeks, Implying Zavah Status Applies
The Calculation: 11 + 14 = 25 Days Maximum Before Birth
The Distinction: 26 Days Would Require Zavah Status; Miscarriage Gets No Kishuy Leniency

macro_segments.title: Bleeding During the 66 Pure Days After Birth: Can Childbirth Pains Make It Permissible?

micro_segments.title: The Tannaitic Dispute: Tanna Kamma (All Blood Permissible) vs. Rabbi Eliezer (Must Be Niddah)
The Rabbis' First Argument: From Lenient to Lenient (Pain vs. Pain Before Birth)
Rabbi Eliezer's Response: The Dayo Principle—Pain Cannot Exceed Its Source (Zavah Only)
The Rabbis' Second Argument: From Lenient to Lenient (Non-Pain vs. Pain After Birth)
Rabbi Eliezer's Logical Basis: Blood from Self (Permitted) vs. Blood from Fetus (Restricted)
The Resolution: The Torah's Phrase 'Pure from Her Wellspring' Limits Leniency to Natural Bleeding
The Rabbis' Final Answer: Since She Cannot Be Zavah, She Also Cannot Be Niddah
